topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I pulled the stack of envelopes out. My breath hitched in my throat. Tucked beneath a glossy flyer was a card. It was thick, cream-colored cardstock, completely blank save for a single, elegantly scripted line at the bottom: "Happy Birthday." My fingers, usually steady when handling paperwork, trembled slightly as I picked it up. Who had sent this? There was no address, no return stamp. A strange, heavy pressure settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I stared at the card, the silence of my apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I wanted to drop it, to shove it back into the pile, but my hand remained frozen, hovering inches above the paper.

[story 2]

I found it wedged between a utility bill and a junk mail advertisement. The card was pristine, a stark white square with elegant, looping handwriting across the front wishing someone a happy birthday. My gaze darted around the hallway, checking for an observer, though the apartment was empty save for me. A flush crept up my neck, warming my cheeks. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. I picked it up with both hands, feeling the texture of the paper beneath my fingertips, as if it might burn me. *This isn't mine,* a voice whispered in my head, thin and reedy. I turned the card over, searching for any clue, any sign that this was meant for me, and found nothing but the simple, startling declaration.

[story 3]

The sound of the mailbox opening felt deafeningly loud in the quiet afternoon. There, lying amidst the usual clutter of junk mail, was a card. Unaddressed. It felt alien in my hand, smooth and strangely warm. I unfolded it slowly, my eyes scanning the space where a sender’s name should have been. A quickening sensation began in my stomach, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action difficult. I glanced toward the front door, expecting someone to materialize, but the hallway remained deserted. I carefully folded the card, the movement jerky and uncertain, and tucked it deep into the pocket of my cardigan, needing the physical barrier between me and the unexpected item.
